The study did not involve the use of a control group.

o: Earning good grades in college causes higher incomes in adulthood.
Good grades in college and high incomes are related.
Half of the income of employees who are age 30 can be explained by their college grades.
There is no relationship between college grades and income at age 30.

Question Instructions: Your response to the question should be provided in six parts: A, B, C, D, E, and F. Write the response to each part of the question in complete sentences. Use appropriate psychological terminology in your response. Source Introduction In the 1950s and 1960s, Stanley Milgram, a psychologist at Yale University, led a series of studies that tested whether people would obey the commands of a researcher in a lab. The study presented is a replication of one of Milgram’s original studies. Participants Individuals who responded to advertisements and flyers went through a series of screening procedures to eliminate people who had taken more than two psychology courses in college; been diagnosed with, or had therapy for, a psychological disorder; abused alcohol or drugs; had medical conditions affected by stress; or experienced severe trauma. The final sample had 29 men and 41 women. The participants ranged in age from 20 to 81 (mean age = 42.9 years, standard deviation = 15.67, median age = 41). Participant education and race/ethnicity demographics are presented in the table. Education and Ethnicity Number of Participants Percentage of Participants Education High school or less 12 17.1 Some college 16 22.9 Bachelor’s degree 28 40.0 Master’s degree 14 20.0 Ethnicity White 38 54.3 Asian 19 26.7 Latino/a/Hispanic 9 12.9 African American 3 4.3 Did not state 1 1.4 A committee of professionals reviewed the study to ensure the appropriateness of the study before researchers began. Method Participants were randomly assigned to one of two conditions: the “base” condition and the “modeled refusal” condition. The participants agreed to be in the study and knew they could withdraw at any time and keep the $50 they were paid to participate. In the base condition, the participant was paired with a “learner,” who was a research confederate. The participant read 25 different word-pairs (e.g., strong–arm) that the “learner” had to recall. If the “learner” gave an incorrect answer, the participant administered an electric shock using a “shock generator” that had switches ranging from 15 to 450 volts in 15-volt increments. The shock was fake, but the participant did not know this. The. The participant moved up one switch on the shock generator for each incorrect answer. While the participants watched, the researcher strapped the “learners” into a chair with electrodes. The participant then went to another room for the remainder of the study. The “learners” gave correct and incorrect responses according to a predetermined schedule. After participants pressed the 75-volt switch, they heard a small grunt from the “learner.” The grunts got louder after each successive shock. When a participant pressed the 150-volt switch, the “learner” yelled, “Ugh. Get me out of here. I told you I had heart trouble. My heart’s starting to bother me now. I refuse to go on. Let me out.” If the participant expressed reluctance or hesitation at any time, the researcher gave one of four directions, in this order: “Please continue”; “The research requires that you continue”; “It is absolutely essential that you continue”; and “You have no other choice, you must continue.” The researcher assessed whether the participants continued to administer shocks after receiving each direction. The base condition ended when the participant refused to continue or agreed to continue at the shock level just past 150 volts. In the modeled refusal condition, the procedures were the same as the base condition except for an additional confederate who was paired with each participant. The men were paired with a White man in his 50s. Women were paired with a White woman in her late 20s. The researcher pretended to randomly choose the participant to be Teacher 2 and the additional confederate to be Teacher 1. Teacher 1 administered shocks to the “learner” according to the procedures as the participant watched. At the 90-volt shock level, Teacher 1 said to the researcher, “I don’t know about this.” The researcher responded with “Please continue.” Teacher 1 paused and then said, “I don’t think I can do this,” and pushed their chair from the table. The researcher then asked Teacher 2 (the participant) to continue the test, picking up where Teacher 1 left off. As with the base condition, if the participant (Teacher 2) expressed reluctance or hesitation, the researcher gave one of the four directions as described. The researcher assessed whether the participants continued to administer shocks after receiving each direction. The modeled refusal condition also ended when the participant refused to continue or agreed to continue at the shock level just past 150 volts. When the study was over, the researcher explained informed participants of the true purpose of the study after the study was complete, specifically that the “learner” was not receiving electric shocks. The “learner” entered the lab room at that point to assure the participant that they were fine. Sources for Review: 1 2 3 Source 1 Introduction In this study, researchers explored the relationship between eating spicy food and having aggressive thoughts. The researchers conducted an experiment in which they predicted that spicy food consumption would cause participants to have more aggressive thoughts when compared with participants who did not eat spicy food. Participants Sixty college students participated in the experiment. The researchers did not provide additional information about the participants’ race or gender identities. Method Participants were randomly assigned to eat either a plain tortilla chip (non-spicy condition) or a tortilla chip with two drops of habanero pepper1 sauce on it (spicy condition). Participants then rated how spicy the tortilla chip was, how much they liked it, and their general preference for eating spicy foods. These ratings were made on 7-point Likert scales, with higher ratings indicating greater spiciness, greater enjoyment, and greater preference for spicy foods, respectively. Next, the participants completed a word fragment quiz. In the quiz, they were presented with a series of word fragments and were asked to choose one of two provided options to complete the fragment. One of the choices to complete the fragment was associated with aggression while the other choice was not. For example, for the fragment “H_T,” participants could choose “HAT” or “HIT.” Across the series of word fragments, participants received a score of 0 to 10 for the number of aggressive options they chose. Finally, participants were asked to read a brief story about a character whose behavior could be perceived as having aggressive or non-aggressive intentions. They then reported the degree to which they believed the intent of the character’s behavior was aggressive, using a 7-point Likert scale, where higher ratings indicated greater perceived aggression intentions. Results and Discussion Participants in the spicy condition were more likely to complete word fragments with an aggressive option (mean = 5.58; standard deviation = 1.88) than were participants in the non-spicy condition (mean = 3.75; standard deviation = 1.86). The researchers also found that participants in the spicy condition were more likely to attribute the story character’s behavior to aggressive intentions (mean = 5.45; standard deviation = .86) than were those in the non-spicy condition (mean = 4.71; standard deviation = .98). All differences reported were statistically significant. Batra, R. K., Ghoshal, T., & Raghunathan, R. (2017). Use appropriate psychological terminology. Sources for Review: 1 2 3 Source 1 Introduction Psychologists were interested in how middle school and high school students experienced burnout in schools. In this study, the researchers examined the ways in which students’ reports of experiencing burnout were associated with school-related demands and resources. Participants The researchers collected data from 696 students in two secondary schools in the nation of Hungary. Of the students, 66% percent of the students were male and 34% were female.1 The participants’ average age was 16.2 years, and all students and parents agreed to participate in the study. Researchers did not report race/ethnicity data beyond nation of origin for participants. Method Participants responded to two quantitative measures collected electronically through a website. The first measure on student burnout asked questions that were grouped into three components of burnout: feelings of personal inadequacy at school (such as worrying about whether one will be successful in school), exhaustion at school (such as worrying about school during non-school hours), and cynicism toward school (such as loss of interest in school). The second measure examined students’ perceptions of the demands and resources of their schools. “Demands” referred to the mental challenges that students face (such as conflicts with teachers or other authorities), work style (such as the time of day a student completes schoolwork), and other emotional burdens related to school (such as worry and fear). “Resources” referred to social support in helping students develop feelings of autonomy or to discover opportunities to develop personally. All questionnaire items were answered on six-point Likert scales, with higher scores indicating more burnout. Results and Discussion In the first measure, the researchers found that female participants reported significantly higher levels of feelings of inadequacy and exhaustion than did male participants. No statistically significant difference in average scores for cynicism toward school was found. The means and standard deviations for each category are listed in Table 1. Sources for Review: 1 2 3 Source 1 Introduction In this study, researchers investigated whether the Big Five personality traits were related to compulsive buying. Compulsive buying is a chronic tendency to purchase things that provide short-term rewards. However, compulsive buying results in longer-term negative consequences to the person buying the items and to those they care about. Researchers were interested in whether the Big Five personality traits were related to compulsive buying. Participants The sample included 1,093 university students from the nation of Spain, ranging in age from 18 to 23 years old (mean age = 20.5 years). There were 571 female and 522 male participants.1 In this sample, 1,007 participants were non-compulsive buyers, and 86 were compulsive buyers. No other race/ethnicity data beyond national origin were reported by the researchers. Method To indicate how much they engaged in compulsive buying, participants first responded to a 16-question self-report survey that included questions such as, “I often buy things just because they’re cheap.” Participants used a response range of 1 (strongly disagree) to 4 (strongly agree). Higher scores indicated a stronger tendency to engage in compulsive buying. To show how much they displayed each of the Big Five personality traits, participants then responded to a 44-question self-report survey. In the Big Five Survey, participants used a response range of 1 (strongly disagree) to 5 (strongly agree). Higher scores indicated a higher degree of each of the traits. Results and Discussion Researchers compiled and analyzed the data from compulsive buyers and non-compulsive buyers for each Big Five trait.2 Results from the analyses appear in the graph. Comparison of Compulsive and Non-Compulsive Buyers The differences between compulsive and non-compulsive buyers were statistically significant for the traits of neuroticism, agreeableness, and conscientiousness, but not for extraversion or openness. Introduction This study examined whether an adolescent's selfie1-related behaviors might relate to their concerns about their physical appearance. Participants Participants consisted of students from two middle schools and two high schools in China. In total, 767 students completed a series of surveys at two data collection times. Of the 767 students, 384 were women and 383 were men.2 The mean age of all the students in that sample was 15.78 years. All adult and minor participants agreed to be in the study and knew they could back out at any time. Researchers ensured that the individual participants could not be identified from the data collected. No other race/ethnicity data were reported by the researchers beyond national origin. Method Data were collected at two points in time during regularly scheduled class meetings. The data on the first set of variables was collected in March, while the data on the second set of variables was collected in September of the same year. To measure the students’ selfie-related behaviors, researchers had the students complete surveys on the selfie-posting, selfie-viewing, and selfie-editing variables: For selfie-posting, the students were asked how often they posted selfies on social media. The survey response range for students was 1 (very infrequently) to 6 (several times a day). For selfie-viewing, the students were asked how frequently they looked at selfies posted by other people on social media. The survey response range was 1 (very infrequently) to 6 (several times a day). For selfie-editing, the students were asked whether and how often they used at least one of three techniques to improve their physical appearance in selfies before posting them on social media: (1) cropping or cutting parts of themselves out of their selfies; (2) using photographic filters; and/or (3) using photo editing software or applications. The survey response range for each technique was 1 (never) to 5 (always). To measure appearance-related concerns, researchers had the students complete surveys on the self-objectification,3 facial dissatisfaction, and body dissatisfaction variables: For self-objectification, the students responded to 4 prompts such as “During the day, I think about how I look many times,” using a survey response range of 1 (strongly disagree) to 7 (strongly agree). For facial dissatisfaction, the students answered 11 prompts in which they indicated their level of dissatisfaction with their own facial features. The survey response range was 0 (never) to 4 (always). Higher scores indicated more facial dissatisfaction. For body dissatisfaction, the students indicated their level of dissatisfaction with nine physical features of their own bodies. The survey response range was a 1 (very satisfied) to 5 (very dissatisfied). Results and Discussion The table lists the relationships between each type of selfie-related behavior and each type of appearance concern. Notwithstanding two exceptions, all the selfie-related behaviors and all the appearance concerns had a positive relationship. The table lists the strength and direction of the relationships between the variables in the study. Relationships between Variables in the Study Variables March: Selfie-Posting March: Selfie-Viewing March: Selfie-Editing September: Self-Objectification 0.25* 0.36* 0.37* September: Facial Dissatisfaction 0.11* 0.21* 0.23* September: Body Dissatisfaction 0.01 - 0.01 0.14* *An asterisk indicates that the statistic is statistically significant. These findings suggest that an adolescent’s selfie-related behaviors are more closely linked to their self-objectification and facial dissatisfaction than to their overall body dissatisfaction. Wang, Y., Xie, X., Fardouly, J., Vartanian, L. R., & Lei, L. (2021). Sources for Review: 1 2 3 Source 1 Introduction In this study, researchers examined whether participants’ acceptance of an apology depended on whether the participants believed an offense was intentional or accidental. Participants The researchers recruited 47 undergraduate students to participate in the study. The participants’ average age was 22 years old. The researchers did not report the participants’ gender or race/ethnicity data. The participants provided informed consent prior to the study, and they were debriefed about the study before they departed the laboratory. Method When a participant arrived at the laboratory, researchers paired the participant with a confederate who posed as another participant but was working with the researchers. Both the participant and the confederate were seated at a computer equipped with a keyboard and joystick. Researchers asked both students to read an article and then answer 10 questions about it. One of the students said their answers to the questions out loud while the other student typed the answers into the computer. The procedure was set up so that the confederate was always assigned to type the answers. Before the participant and the confederate began reading the article and answering the questions, researchers informed them that touching the joystick would immediately and permanently delete any data they had entered into the computer. If the data were deleted, neither the participant nor the research assistant would receive tickets to enter a raffle to win $50. Prior to the study, researchers had instructed the confederate to vary their behavior based on their experimental group: In Group 1, the confederate accidentally touched the joystick as he was reaching for an eyeglass case. The confederate in this group apologized for touching the joystick. In Group 2, the confederate accidentally touched the joystick as he was reaching for an eyeglass case. The confederate did not apologize for touching the joystick. In Group 3, the confederate intentionally touched the joystick, stating that he had a similar joystick at home and was unconcerned about the researchers’ instructions. The confederate apologized for touching the joystick. In Group 4, the confederate intentionally touched the joystick, stating that he had a similar joystick at home and was unconcerned about the researchers’ instructions. The confederate did not apologize for touching the joystick. The confederate then tried to recover the data but was unsuccessful. For all experimental groups, a researcher returned and, in a frustrated manner, told the participant and the confederate that without the data, they would not be given tickets to enter the raffle to win $50. The researcher then interviewed the participant to learn more about the participant’s experiences during the study. During the interview, the participant was asked to rate the confederate’s level of intent to touch the joystick and about the quality of their apology. The researcher told the participant that because the confederate is the only one who touched the joystick, the participant would receive five tickets for the raffle. The researcher allowed the participant to distribute the five raffle tickets however they wished, to themselves or to the confederate. The researchers also had participants complete a survey about their attitude toward the confederate, on a scale of 1 (not at all favorable) to 7 (very favorable). Results and Discussion The researchers recorded how many tickets the participants shared with the confederate. Of the confederates who apologized, the ones who accidentally touched the joystick received more tickets than the ones who intentionally touched the joystick. When the confederate had intentionally touched the joystick, an apology did not lead to them receiving more tickets. In fact, confederates who intentionally touched the joystick and did not apologize received more tickets than the confederates who intentionally touched the joystick and apologized. Struthers, C.
